misconceptionSummary: Students divide the whole parts and decimal parts separately rather than converting to an equivalent whole-number division.

lines.text: Split the division — treated parts independently

steps.narration: The student tried to split three point six and zero point four into separate parts and divide them independently. Division does not work this way — the whole expression is a single ratio and must be treated as one.

visuals.ruleText: Multiply BOTH numbers by the same power of 10 to clear all decimals

steps.narration: Multiply both three point six and zero point four by ten. Three point six becomes thirty-six and zero point four becomes four. Now divide thirty-six by four — a straightforward whole-number calculation.

Thirty-six divided by four is nine. Check: zero point four times nine is three point six — correct. The estimate confirms the scale: three point six divided by about half gives roughly seven, so nine is plausible.

visuals.label: Treat as one ratio — scale both by ×10, then divide whole numbers

steps.narration: Nine. Always treat a division as a single ratio — scale both values by the same power of ten, then divide as whole numbers.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: splitting 3.6 ÷ 0.4 and dividing parts separately. Treat it as one ratio — multiply both by 10: 36 ÷ 4 = 9. Check: 0.4 × 9 = 3.6 ✓
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: A decimal division is a single ratio — never split the digits and divide separately. Multiply both the dividend and divisor by the same power of ten, then do one clean whole-number division.
